speaker: interviewee
text: After I went into the door, which was pointed-- he pointed me to, I looked back. And I was very sad that I had to be separated from my mother. And my mother looked back, and we waved. And that's the last time that I saw her. I went to the group that it was before me, and I knew some of the girls there. And I was happy that I knew somebody. But I was very sad that I still was alone without my family. Then after a while, my younger sister, who gave her child to my mother, came running. And we embraced, and we kissed, and we hugged, and the two of us was together. So it was a little relief that I wasn't alone. We went to a big hall where we had to get undressed. And they took-- took us in another room. We had to shower. And they gave us gray uniform dresses. We were not allowed to go back to take anything from our own clothes or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just left them in the hall?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just left them in that hall. I also carried with me a briefcase with my-- with our citizenship-- with our citizen papers because the first ones who were deported from our city was those who came from different countries and they were not citizens. To prove that we are citizens, proudly, we took that with us but we left it there. They shaved our hair completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shaved your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other prisoners or guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were prisoners, yes. They were prisoners who were there before us. And we begged them not to, but they didn't-- they didn't help. It came to a point that we looked at each other, and we were laughing. We looked like-- everybody looked the same. My sister hardly recognized me. She said she recognized me because I looked like my uncle. We looked-- there was a window and we looked at ourselves in the window. We looked strange. We didn't recognize ourselves. Then they lined us up. And they tooked us to the camp, which was Camp B in Auschwitz. They were alphabetically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had shoes, yeah. We had our own shoes. That they left. They gave us the shoes. And there were barracks with wooden-- with wooden beds, uh, bunk beds. And we had to-- I was on the top, so we had to climb up. And there was a lot of us on a very small space.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were mothers with daughters, which they must have been in their 40s, early 40s, late 40s, not older. And the girls, my age and older. And when we went to sleep, we didn't know how. There was nothing. It was bare. And we had no room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was on another-- on the lower bed next to me-- not under me, next to me. And we were hysterical laughing. And they gave us something to drink before we went into the bunks, which was like black coffee. But later, we found out that they put something into that-- [? Brom, ?] which is like, uh, a tranquilizer, something like that. The name was [? Brom. ?] I don't know-- in German maybe? And we were hysterical laughing. We were like, like monkeys. We didn't know what's happening. And, and, it was-- it was a very, very funny situation. And we had to go to sleep and we couldn't find room. We, we couldn't-- we didn't know how to do it. We couldn't sit up and we couldn't lay down. But we managed. We squeezed together. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people on a--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- I don't know, maybe 30. I don't think-- I don't even-- I don't remember but I know it was very crowded. And then I was sleeping next to, um, a-- a-- a girl, whose mother was next to her. One side was the mother and one side was the daughter. The mother, to make more room for the child, she used to push me, push me, push me. And she pinched me. She gave-- because she wanted her daughter to be more comfortable. And it was miserable feeling. In the morning, they woke us up. It was dark yet. Must have been maybe four o'clock in the morning. They lined us up. And they gave us a speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to obey everything what we say you have to do. And if you not, you see those chimneys? He says, you're going to be burned there. Who told-- who was telling this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Jewish girls who were there from before us. But they had orders to treat us this way because they were treated that way, too. So they-- this-- they had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, and you have no parents no more. You know that? They were all burned last night in the crematorium. And I said, how could you say such thing? How could you be so cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to believe it. We just thought that she's very mean, and she just has the authority to scare us. We didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, this was-- so we stayed in line. And they gave us some black coffee and a piece of very, very black, very heavy bread but we couldn't eat. We says, this is not food. We're not going to eat this. We can't digest this. How could we? This is not for human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, we got hungry. We ate it. We didn't have enough from it. They gave us once a day, a little slice. And that had to be. And the suppertime-- lunchtime, also something very dark liquid, and again, first, before supper, it was suppertime. And that was the day we stood in line most of the time. We went to the bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked between us, you know, the girls, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: About what?

speaker: interviewee
text: About well, in the beginning, what's going to be? And then we went to the past and everybody had a story about the family, about, you know, different things. There were women who were newlyweds. And there were women who had children, babies, and they were separated. So they were crying all night. Like my sister, she cried all night. She-- she had nightmares and she was thinking about the child. And we didn't want to believe it, but she didn't know what's happened to the child. And it was very, very difficult times. And, and we were there for six weeks. We didn't go to work. We didn't do any work. We were just standing. Even when they had to tell-- Appell, it was called, and they called us to stay. It was raining. It was cold. We had no nothing to cover us, just a dress. And we were tired of standing. So we used to take turns. Something-- somebody from the front came to the back to lay-- to-- to sit down a little bit or to bend down. And, and to put the dress, picked up from the back to cover our shoulders because we were very cold. But they not wasn't supposed to see us. We're not allowed to do it. So we changed. We take turns to go in the back, they shouldn't see us. After six weeks, my sister got very skinny. She was worried. She was crying. She had sleepless night. We had another selection. And we had to stay in line. And they came over, and they looked us over. And they told us to pick up our dress to look at our legs. And my sister was like that, very thin. And she herself was very thin. But we pinched her to have some colors. And we made her dress more bloussant, that she should look that she's not as thin as she is. And they didn't put her into the selection, where the more, who were in more-- in more better condition stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- I was in better condition. They took her to another group. And I said to her, I'm not going to stay here. And I don't care what happens to you. I'm going to be with you. I'm going to go with you. And I sneaked over to her side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were friend of ours from the city who saw what's happening. She said, you know what? We gonna watch out for you. When they're not going to look in this direction, you take your sister by her hand and sneak her over to the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I did. There were SS soldiers standing. There were women, and men. They didn't see. And the distance was a few steps away. And I pulled her, and I went to the other side. I was very happy to be with her. But then, they took us to the station where the wagons were waiting for us to transport us to somewhere else. They had another selection. This time, we had to be naked. You had to strip everything off. And you had to-- one by one, they looked you over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister said to me, what did it help you now? If I didn't get in before now, I'm sure I'm not going to get in. I says, don't worry. Don't worry. See what happens. Maybe a miracle will happen. Maybe we'll, we'll come into the other room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it was they selected us. Even then, some of this was selected to be with the healthier ones were selected out. If somebody had a pimple, or a rash, or something, they didn't select them into that group. But my sister was selected. And we were together again. And we went to Gelsenkirchen. They took us to Gelsenkirchen. We didn't know where we're going to go. We didn't know what's happening to us. We didn't know if it's a good thing that we left Auschwitz. But we felt it was good because over there, we don't, didn't have much hope to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there by wagon trains again. We got to Gelsenkirchen. They gave us clothing. They gave us a blanket. And we were living in soldiers' barracks. The soldiers used to live there, which was very decent. We had a bed. We had something on the bed to sleep on and we had a blanket. And they gave us food also, better food. Slice of bread with a piece of margarine, and better drink. I don't know. Coffee, whatever it was. And they-- we went to work. There, we went to work. In the morning, we lined up early in the morning. And there were trolley cars and we went to factories to clean up after the bombing. It was also a big ammunition factory there, which was for the army. And that's where we worked. And then we went home. We had-- again, we stayed in line. They gave us whatever the ration, food. And we went to sleep, which wasn't bad. The conditions was much better than in Auschwitz. And here, we were kept more like human beings. And, uh, we were there about three months or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went together, we went 2,000. And 500 were separated and we fell into that group. And we went to Essen, which was not far from the city. Essen was a big factory city. Krupp factories were there, which was very famous. And, uh, also the same thing. We cleaned the machines. And we helped out whatever, to straighten out, to, to clean, and to do things there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hours-- we got up every day very early. We stayed in line before we started out to go to work. Must have been around eight o'clock. And then five o'clock or six o'clock, coming back, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us food before we left in the morning. They gave us the bread and the margarine. We came home-- and that was part of our breakfast and, and lunch. And for supper, they gave us a hot soup, like vegetable, whatever. We stood in line again. And then we went to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, did you know what happened to your brother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- yes, we had more of an idea. I knew that my parents will not survive. That age didn't survive, very few of them survived. I know my younger brother also not. But the two older brothers, I'm hoping because they were healthy boys. They were nice looking boys. That they probably-- if I, they selected me, which I was the younger one and more sensitive child, I'm sure they're going to survive. They're probably working where the men are working. And after, whatever-- if we're going to survive, you're going to have some part, somebody from the family living. From-- from Essen, we went to Gelsen-- to Bergen-Belsen. The British were coming closer to Essen, and they didn't want us to survive, I guess. And that was four weeks before the liberation. We were still in good condition. We were still all together. Nobody died from us. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was you sister still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister was. My sister was with me, which was a big advantage. Otherwise, I don't think I would have survived. We helped each other. She was down, I helped her. And we were very close. And whatever we could do for each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger one, the one who had one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillie. And, uh, we were dressed pretty decent. But, but they gave us that we shouldn't run away, they cut out a piece of fabric from the back of the dress or the jacket, like a square piece, and they gave-- put another color that they should know that we are prisoners. That was the sign of a prisoners. That we shouldn't run away. They should catch us. If we run away, they should know that we are not civil people, we're not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever try to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two girls ran away. And they, uh, searched because they counted us every morning, when we stayed in line how many are-- how many we are. And they knew that two was missing. And nobody said anything. We didn't know where they went. The truth, we didn't know. They kept it in secret. And they survived. They never find them, and they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was-- her name was Gisela. Gisela, a young girl. They met, in the factories, Germans who were nice to them and they cooperated with them. And they asked them if they want to run away, they're going to help them. And they did. They took them into their house. And they dressed them. They, you know, they disguised them. From the factory, they didn't go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans in the factory. They met them. They were Germans, who were working on those, uh, uh, machines. And, uh-- and they were liberated. They survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk to the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. One of them was very nice to me because his wife came to visit him once. And she asked me to come in. He wants to-- my wife-- his wife wants to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I reminded him of his daughter. He had a daughter who died in the bombing and I reminded him of the daughter. So he-- he used to give me a little extra food here and there, which was a big, big plus, a little extra.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I don't remember his name. I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the other guards like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other guards were tough but they didn't harm us. They didn't hurt us or they didn't hit us. We went to work, and they were supervising and watching us. They came with us with the guns. One incident I had, which was very scary. I needed shoes because my shoes was just like fell apart. I couldn't wear them anymore. And it was cold. It was winter. It was snow. And I asked for shoes. And they had, between our girls, whose father was a shoemaker who used to patch up-- they have a machine. They used to patch shoes, one on top of the patch on the other. And they were wooden. They were not leather. The top was, uh, material, and the heel was wood. And I went in and I asked for a pair of shoes. And the Lagerfuhrer, which was the head of the camp, saw me going in there and he followed me. And they gave me a shoe which was full of patches. And I could not put my foot in there. I tried and I tried. And he said I'm making believe. It's not true. That has to fit. That shoe has to go on my feet. And I just couldn't. And I said, I can't. He says, you are fooling. You-- you don't want to go to work. And that's why you don't want to put your shoes on. I says, but I do want to go to work. Now, give me another pair of shoes. There's no other pair of shoes. You must fit your foot into that shoe. And he took me my hand and took me out. And he put me against the wall. And he started beating me. He had a rubber stick and he was aiming at my head. And I was, you know, going down, down, down but he was still hitting me. He was hitting me so that I fainted. And then somebody went to tell my sister that what, you know, something happening to, to me, that you should come. And she came running. And I got to my self but it was terrible pain. And I had like, swollen, I think, my head was swollen. And it was already time to go to bed to sleep. I mean, it was late already. So I went, and I couldn't sleep from pain. She put some cold water on it. And we were crying, both of us, all night. But after that, I was very scared. Whenever I saw him, I used to shake. I was so scared of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know his real name, Just Lagerfuhrer. And, uh, nothing happened to anybody. He was like a maniac then, you know? He was like-- he lost control. And he was just-- I don't know. He wanted to kill me or-- ah, it wasn't, you know, much-- if he would have beat me yet a few more times, I think I would have-- that would have been the end. But anyhow, that was an experience. And, uh, then we went to, uh, to, to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- before that, did you have any Jewish holidays? Were you able to celebrate anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, we couldn't. There was a girl whose father was a rabbi. And she was-- she remembered the prayers by heart. And like, we knew when there was a new month, new moon, new month, there's a certain prayer that you say. So she used to say it, and we used to repeat it. And there once was a bombing. And we went down into a cellar but had no doors and no windows. It was open both sides. And the bomb fell near our camp. And you felt the, uh, pressure from the bombs. And nobody wanted to be by the end. Everybody was crowding in the middle. And she said certain prayers before you die. So we repeated it. We said it all. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the guards hear you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they went to the regular-- uh, you know, where they go underground when the bomb comes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Shelters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelters. They went to regular shelters. But we didn't go to the regular shelters. And, uh, we still had hope. We still believed and we still prayed for survival and help. And that was kept us going, didn't give up hope. Those who give up hope, it was bad. Then they didn't care for themselves. And they let go. And those didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have bathrooms and showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- they took us to for shower. There was like a fountain outside of the, uh, bunks, with water. And that's where we washed our-- we washed our dress and put it on wet. And, uh, toilet-- the bombed-- when the bombing was done, they bombed also our bunks. So after that was really very bad. We ran into a cellar, which must have been a storage for fruit with cement floor. And those who were lucky, they had the board, the piece of board to sleep on. If not, they slept on cement. And there was where the water was running down, a sewer. That's where you did what you had to do. That was really very, very hard. That was before we went to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there before Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for a month, a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wintertime. It was-- must have been February.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we heard the, uh, guns, uh, very loud. And we knew that, uh, the enemies, I mean, the British are coming closer. And we were afraid they're not going to let us survive. But we didn't know how they are going to get rid of us. How they are going to kill us or what. I knew they didn't want us to survive and tell the story what happened. So then we went to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also by wagon, cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did everybody go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody went. Everybody went. And, uh, in this wagon train, they gave us over to different soldiers. And between them was a guy from France, a young guy, who had the uniform. And he was very friendly to me, and very nice. And he wanted to make a conversation or something. And I was afraid of him. I didn't trusted him. And I didn't know what he wants. I was a young girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him-- he says, don't worry, whatever. I says, if you are friend-- if you are-- if you want to do something for me, just tell me where are we going and what's going to happen to us? He says, I cannot tell you. I cannot tell you. And he was very sad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, when we got there-- we got there. We saw that it was very, very bad there. The situation, the conditions, was the worst, the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the odor of the camp was very, very heavy. Very bad, very strange odor. And we walked around, and we saw the people were like walking skeletons. They were very skinny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have clothes on?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had clothes on but some of them could hardly walk. Like if you would put them a little push, they would fall and never get up again. They were just dying. We could see that in them. Their days were already, you know, end of their life. And then we saw pile of corpse, like mountains of corpse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Throw one on top of the other. Throw on top. Many, many of them. Hills like, you know, one next to the other, next to the other. And then we saw this is going to be the end. They put us into a room, which was bare cement floor, nothing. And that's how we slept. We put whatever we had under our head. The morning, you got up. You saw people dying who were alive last night. They were dead. They envied us. They said, we are in very good condition and we're going to survive. And the war is near the end. And you're going to be lucky. You're going to be survive. But we're not going to survive anymore. And that's what happened. People were dying left and right. They were dead before us. And there was typhus. And there was a lot of sicknesses and lice. We were infected with lice. I myself, my sister, we tried to clean. We have already hair. Our hair grew back. And it was the worst condition. That was really, really indescribable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do with all the dead people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were buried. They made, like, uh, big graves. And, uh, I didn't even concentrate on that much because, uh, there were so many.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who should do it? The people from our-- they took us to carry the dead bodies into the grave, and, uh, to help them. But some of them are not capable of doing it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was brave. And she always used to go look at the dead. She says maybe she's going to recognize somebody from our family. I couldn't do it. And this went on. The food was also very little, a little something hot, and then slice of bread. And, um, we knew that the war is close because there were rumors that the British are closer and they're coming closer. And there was a wooded area like a forest. And we saw from there. We heard the noise coming with the big bangs, the, the guns. You heard it louder and louder, that this must coming from there. And they are closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we still had hope that we will survive. But then they came closer, like a week before the Germans ran away. The SS ran away and they left us there. And there was nothing to be eaten. No water to drink. It was-- Were there any guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no more guards. There was some Hungarian soldiers, who helped, who came, went voluntarily to the German army. They were around yet. And instead of helping us, they showed their true themselves, the real-- that they were, like, they enjoyed seeing us like this. And they would not help or do anything from us, for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister went where the kitchen used to be. And there were like garbage-- peels of potato and beets. She picked it up. She had a little knife. She scraped up a little meat. Where did she get the knife from?

words.text: Where did she get the knife from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what she was eating. She was eating. And then they had some cans, which was food it it-- milk, whatever. So we took the can from the garbage. And we tried to take drips, whatever was left there to give us something because we were desperate. We were so hungry. And there was no water. Then my sister got very sick. She got the typhus. And I got the typhus, too. And we're really with fever and no water. It was-- we thought this is the end. But then the British army came and liberated us, which was in April. April 15.
